What will we need to do in this repository to publish to NPM under the assertlabs NPM namespace (that is the name of the org we have there)? Something like @assertlabs/cli or @assertlabs/assert. Once we do that, we will add badges to the top of the readme with https://img.shields.io/npm/v/@assertlabs/cli.svg and https://img.shields.io/npm/l/@assertlabs/cli.svg, for example. Documentation reflects installation via `npm install -g @assert-labs/cli`, which is the GitHub repository.

Assert 5

6

7 Share session data from any coding agent. 8
9 About 10 · 11 Installation 12 · 13 Supported Agents 14 · 15 Documentation 16

17

18 19 20 21

22

23 24 ## About 25 26 Capture AI agent sessions from any agentic coding tool as part of your repository's history and reference them during code review. 27 28 ## How It Works 29 30 1. **Global hooks** are installed to each agent's config directory. 31 2. When an agent session starts, a new JSONL file is created in `.sessions/`. 32 3. All events (prompts, tool calls, responses) are appended to the session file. 33 34 ## Installation 35 36 > [!NOTE] 37 > Right now, only MacOS/Linux operating systems are supported. 38 39 **Native Install (Recommended):** 40 41 ```bash 42 curl https://assert.dev/install -fsS | bash 43 ``` 44 45 **Homebrew:** 46 47 ```bash 48 brew install assert-labs/tap/assert 49 ``` 50 51 **NPM:** 52 53 ```bash 54 npm install -g @assert-labs/cli 55 ``` 56 57 **From Source:** 58 59 ```bash 60 git clone https://github.com/Assert-Labs/cli.git 61 cd cli 62 pnpm install 63 pnpm build 64 npm install -g . 65 ``` 66 67 ### Initializing Hooks 68 69 ```bash 70 # Install hooks for all supported agents 71 assert install 72 ``` 73 74 ### Requirements 75 76 - macOS or Linux, x64 or arm64 (no Windows or Alpine/musl build yet) 77 - `git` available on your PATH — the CLI shells out to git at runtime 78 - If installing via NPM or from source: `Node.js 18 or later` 79 80 ## Supported Agents 81 82 | Agent | Plugin Location | 83 | ----------- | --------------------------------- | 84 | Claude Code | `~/.claude/skills/assert/` | 85 | Cursor | `~/.cursor/plugins/local/assert/` | 86 87 - Support for Codex, Devin, OpenCode, Pi, Amp, and more is upcoming. 88 - If you would like support to be added for a particular agent, take a look at [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) and look to see if that agent will be added soon in open [issues](https://github.com/Assert-Labs/cli/issues) and [pull requests](https://github.com/Assert-Labs/cli/pulls). 89 90 ## Commands 91 92 ```bash 93 assert install [agent] # Install hooks globally (all agents if none specified) 94 assert sessions # List sessions in current directory 95 assert show # Show session details 96 assert trace [ref] # Export agent-trace attribution for a revision (default HEAD) 97 assert status # Show current status 98 assert disable # Pause capture (hooks stay installed) 99 assert enable # Resume capture 100 assert help # Show help 101 ``` 102 103 ## Controlling Capture 104 105 Session data is written into a repo's `.sessions/` as the agent works, so it 106 shows up in `git status` like any other file — you stage and commit it yourself. 107 108 - **Skip files:** add a `.assertignore` to the repo root (gitignore-style 109 patterns, e.g. `dist/`, `*.log`). Changes that only touch ignored paths won't 110 trigger capture or appear in session data. 111 - **Turn off persistently:** `assert disable` pauses capture (hooks stay 112 installed) until you run `assert enable`. `assert status` shows the current 113 state. 114 - **Turn off for one session:** set `ASSERT_DISABLE=1` in the environment your 115 agent runs in. 116 117 ## Agent Trace 118 119 Captured sessions can be exported as [Agent Trace](https://agent-trace.dev) 120 records — an open standard for AI code attribution 121 ([spec & reference](https://github.com/cursor/agent-trace)). `assert trace` 122 derives a conformant `TraceRecord` for a revision from your committed session 123 data (attributing lines to the contributing model), so any tool can consume the 124 attribution: 125 126 ```bash 127 assert trace # agent-trace record for HEAD 128 assert trace # for a specific commit 129 ``` 130 131 ## License 132 133 This repository is licensed under the [MIT License](https://github.com/assert-labs/cli/blob/main/LICENSE) 134

1	name: release
2	
3	# Builds standalone single-executable (SEA) binaries for each OS/arch and
4	# publishes them to a GitHub Release on every v* tag.
5	#
6	# Cross-compilation note: Node SEA embeds the *host* node binary, so we cannot
7	# cross-compile — each target needs a matching runner (arm64 macOS = macos-14,
8	# x64 macOS = macos-15-intel, arm64 Linux = ubuntu-24.04-arm). arm64 Linux runners
9	# are free for public repos.
10	#
11	# macOS signing: we ad-hoc sign (`codesign --sign -`) because Apple Silicon
12	# refuses to run a binary whose signature postject invalidated. That is enough
13	# for the `curl | bash` install path — curl does NOT set the com.apple.quarantine
14	# attribute, so Gatekeeper never inspects the binary. Developer ID signing +
15	# notarization is only needed if you later add a browser/.dmg/.pkg download; to
16	# enable it, replace the ad-hoc `codesign --sign -` with a Developer ID identity
17	# imported from secrets and add a notarytool step.
18	
19	on:
20	  push:
21	    tags:
22	      - "v*"
23	
24	permissions:
25	  contents: write
26	
27	jobs:
28	  build:
29	    name: build ${{ matrix.target }}
30	    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
31	    strategy:
32	      fail-fast: false
33	      matrix:
34	        include:
35	          - { os: macos-14, target: darwin-arm64 }
36	          - { os: macos-15-intel, target: darwin-x64 }
37	          - { os: ubuntu-latest, target: linux-x64 }
38	          - { os: ubuntu-24.04-arm, target: linux-arm64 }
39	    steps:
40	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
41	
42	      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
43	
44	      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
45	        with:
46	          node-version: "22"
47	          cache: pnpm
48	
49	      - name: Install dependencies
50	        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
51	
52	      - name: Bundle CLI to CommonJS
53	        run: pnpm build:sea
54	
55	      - name: Generate SEA blob
56	        run: node --experimental-sea-config sea-config.json
57	
58	      - name: Create binary from node + inject blob
59	        shell: bash
60	        run: |
61	          set -euo pipefail
62	          # Copy the running node binary to use as the executable host.
63	          node -e "require('fs').copyFileSync(process.execPath, 'assert')"
64	          # The copied node binary may lack the owner-write bit, which makes
65	          # postject fail with "Can't read and write to target executable".
66	          chmod u+w assert
67	
68	          FUSE="NODE_SEA_FUSE_fce680ab2cc467b6e072b8b5df1996b2"
69	          if [[ "${{ runner.os }}" == "macOS" ]]; then
70	            # Strip the existing signature before injecting, then re-sign ad-hoc.
71	            codesign --remove-signature assert
72	            npx --yes postject assert NODE_SEA_BLOB dist/sea/sea-prep.blob \
73	              --sentinel-fuse "$FUSE" \
74	              --macho-segment-name NODE_SEA
75	            codesign --sign - assert
76	            codesign --verify --verbose assert
77	          else
78	            npx --yes postject assert NODE_SEA_BLOB dist/sea/sea-prep.blob \
79	              --sentinel-fuse "$FUSE"
80	          fi
81	
82	          chmod +x assert
83	          mv assert "assert-${{ matrix.target }}"
84	
85	      - name: Smoke test the binary
86	        # Only run binaries matching the host arch (always true here since each
87	        # target has a native runner). `help` exits 0 on this CLI.
88	        shell: bash
89	        run: ./assert-${{ matrix.target }} help
90	
91	      - name: Upload binary artifact
92	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
93	        with:
94	          name: assert-${{ matrix.target }}
95	          path: assert-${{ matrix.target }}
96	          if-no-files-found: error
97	
98	  release:
99	    name: publish release
100	    needs: build
101	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
102	    steps:
103	      - name: Checkout (for the Homebrew formula template)
104	        uses: actions/checkout@v4
105	
106	      - name: Download all binaries
107	        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
108	        with:
109	          path: artifacts
110	          merge-multiple: true
111	
112	      - name: Generate checksums
113	        working-directory: artifacts
114	        run: sha256sum assert-* > SHA256SUMS
115	
116	      - name: Create GitHub Release
117	        env:
118	          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
119	        run: |
120	          set -euo pipefail
121	          gh release create "${GITHUB_REF_NAME}" \
122	            --repo "${GITHUB_REPOSITORY}" \
123	            --title "${GITHUB_REF_NAME}" \
124	            --generate-notes \
125	            artifacts/assert-* artifacts/SHA256SUMS
126	
127	      - name: Update Homebrew tap
128	        # Renders formula-templates/assert.rb with the release version and the
129	        # per-platform sha256s from SHA256SUMS, then pushes it to the tap repo.
130	        env:
131	          TAP_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN }}
132	          VERSION: ${{ github.ref_name }}
133	        run: |
134	          set -euo pipefail
135	          VER="${VERSION#v}"
136	
137	          sha() {
138	            local v
139	            v="$(awk -v f="$1" '$2 == f { print $1 }' artifacts/SHA256SUMS)"
140	            [[ -n "$v" ]] || { echo "::error::missing sha256 for $1 in SHA256SUMS" >&2; exit 1; }
141	            printf '%s' "$v"
142	          }
143	          SHA_DARWIN_ARM64="$(sha assert-darwin-arm64)"
144	          SHA_DARWIN_X64="$(sha assert-darwin-x64)"
145	          SHA_LINUX_ARM64="$(sha assert-linux-arm64)"
146	          SHA_LINUX_X64="$(sha assert-linux-x64)"
147	
148	          git clone --depth 1 \
149	            "https://x-access-token:${TAP_TOKEN}@github.com/Assert-Labs/homebrew-tap.git" tap
150	
151	          mkdir -p tap/Formula
152	          sed \
153	            -e "s/__VERSION__/${VER}/g" \
154	            -e "s/__SHA_DARWIN_ARM64__/${SHA_DARWIN_ARM64}/g" \
155	            -e "s/__SHA_DARWIN_X64__/${SHA_DARWIN_X64}/g" \
156	            -e "s/__SHA_LINUX_ARM64__/${SHA_LINUX_ARM64}/g" \
157	            -e "s/__SHA_LINUX_X64__/${SHA_LINUX_X64}/g" \
158	            formula-templates/assert.rb > tap/Formula/assert.rb
159	
160	          cd tap
161	          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
162	          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
163	          git add Formula/assert.rb
164	          if git diff --cached --quiet; then
165	            echo "Formula already up to date."
166	            exit 0
167	          fi
168	          git commit -m "assert ${VER}"
169	          git push
170

text:
I've got the full picture. Here's what I found before laying out the plan:

- **Nothing is published yet** — `@assertlabs/cli`, `@assertlabs/assert`, and the current `@assert-labs/cli` are all 404 on npm.
- **`package.json` name is `@assert-labs/cli`** (with a hyphen) — but your org is `assertlabs` (no hyphen), so the name *must* change regardless.
- **The release workflow (`release.yml`) has no `npm publish` step** — it only builds SEA binaries → GitHub Releases and updates the Homebrew tap. NPM publishing doesn't exist anywhere yet.
- Packaging itself is basically ready: `files: ["dist","bin"]`, `bin.assert`, `main`, and a `prepublishOnly: pnpm build` are all in place.

Two decisions are genuinely yours before I can give you exact strings to paste:

questions.question: Which package name should we publish under the assertlabs npm org?

options.label: @assertlabs/cli
options.description: Matches the GitHub repo name (Assert-Labs/cli) and the existing README. Install: npm i -g @assertlabs/cli. Most consistent with repo/docs convention.

options.label: @assertlabs/assert
options.description: Matches the brand and the binary command (`assert`) + Homebrew formula. Reads slightly redundant ('assertlabs assert') but most memorable.

questions.question: How should the npm publish happen?

options.label: Automate in release.yml
options.description: Add a publish-npm job to the existing v* tag workflow using an NPM automation token + provenance. One tag publishes binaries, Homebrew, AND npm together.

options.label: Manual npm publish
options.description: You run `npm publish --access public` locally for now; we just fix the name/config. Simpler, but a second manual step per release.

output: Your questions have been answered: "Which package name should we publish under the assertlabs npm org?"="How can I determine if @assert-labs is available on npm?", "How should the npm publish happen?"="Automate in release.yml". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

text:
Good question — and there's an important distinction buried in it. Let me both answer "how to check" and actually run the checks.

On npm, a **scope** (`@assert-labs` or `@assertlabs`) maps 1:1 to either an **org** or a **user account** of that exact name. So "is `@assert-labs` available" really means "is the org/user name `assert-labs` unregistered." Here's how to check, run live:

input.command:
cd /tmp
for name in assert-labs assertlabs; do
  echo "===== $name ====="
  echo "-- org page (200=org exists) --"
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" "https://www.npmjs.com/org/$name"
  echo "-- user page (200=user exists) --"
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" "https://www.npmjs.com/~$name"
  echo "-- npm org ls (needs auth/membership) --"
  npm org ls "$name" 2>&1 | head -2
done
input.description: Probe npm for org/user name registration

output:
===== assert-labs =====
-- org page (200=org exists) --
403
-- user page (200=user exists) --
403
-- npm org ls (needs auth/membership) --
===== assertlabs =====
-- org page (200=org exists) --
403
-- user page (200=user exists) --
403
-- npm org ls (needs auth/membership) --
Shell cwd was reset to /Users/devin/Desktop/Desktop - Devin MacBook Pro/Files/Assert-Labs/Repos

text:
The website returns `403` to `curl` (bot protection), so that probe is inconclusive — and `npm org ls` needs you to be logged in. So the live check didn't give a definitive answer for the hyphenated name. Here's the reliable way to check, plus why I don't think you need to.

## How to definitively check scope availability

A scope is just an org/user name, so "is `@assert-labs` free?" = "is the org `assert-labs` unregistered?" The authoritative checks all require being logged in (`npm login` first):

1. **For the org you already own:** `npm org ls assertlabs` — lists members if you control it. This confirms `@assertlabs` is yours and ready.
2. **For the hyphenated name:** `npm org ls assert-labs`
   - `403 / "you do not have permission"` → the name exists but isn't yours (taken).
   - a not-found style error → likely available to register.
3. **The definitive test for any scope:** `npm publish --access public --dry-run` won't check ownership, but a real `npm publish` returns **403** if the scope isn't yours and **409** if that exact version already exists. The 403-vs-success on first publish *is* the ownership check.
4. **In the browser** (most reliable, bypasses the curl 403): visit `npmjs.com/org/assert-labs` while logged in, or go to **npmjs.com/org/create** and type `assert-labs` — it tells you immediately if it's taken.

## But you probably shouldn't care about `@assert-labs`

You already own the **`assertlabs`** org (no hyphen). The hyphenated `@assert-labs` in the current `package.json` and README is just a mismatch with the org you actually have. Unless you specifically want to *also* register an `assert-labs` org for the hyphenated brand, the clean path is to align everything to **`@assertlabs`** — the scope you control — and not chase `@assert-labs` availability at all.

So the real fork is still just `@assertlabs/cli` vs `@assertlabs/assert`. You didn't pick one — want me to go with **`@assertlabs/cli`** (matches the `Assert-Labs/cli` repo and current README structure)? That's my recommendation.

---

## The full plan (publish flow = automate in release.yml, which you chose)

Once the name is settled, here's everything this repo needs:

**1. `package.json`**
- Rename `"name": "@assert-labs/cli"` → `"@assertlabs/cli"`.
- Add `"publishConfig": { "access": "public", "provenance": true }` — scoped packages are private-by-default; `access: public` is required to publish free, and provenance adds the supply-chain attestation badge.
- Add `"repository"`, `"homepage"`, and `"bugs"` fields (npm shows these and provenance requires `repository`).

**2. `release.yml` — add a `publish-npm` job** (runs after `build`, on the same `v*` tag):
- `actions/setup-node@v4` with `registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org` (this writes the `.npmrc` auth line).
- `permissions: { id-token: write, contents: read }` on the job — required for npm provenance via OIDC.
- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` → `pnpm build` → `npm publish --provenance --access public`, with `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}`.

**3. Repo secret** — create an **npm automation token** (npmjs.com → Access Tokens → Granular/Automation, bypasses 2FA in CI) and add it as the `NPM_TOKEN` secret in the `Assert-Labs/cli` GitHub repo settings.

**4. Version sync** — your tags drive releases (`v*`), but `npm publish` reads `package.json` `version`. Either bump `package.json` before tagging, or have the workflow set the version from the tag. Worth deciding so npm and the GitHub release never drift.

**5. README + docs**
- Change install line to `npm install -g @assertlabs/cli` (currently `@assert-labs/cli`).
- Add the badges you mentioned, pointed at the real name:
  - `https://img.shields.io/npm/v/@assertlabs/cli.svg`
  - `https://img.shields.io/npm/l/@assertlabs/cli.svg`
- Update the docs site (the `docs/` repo / docs.assert.dev) the same way.

Want me to (a) confirm `@assertlabs/cli`, then (b) make edits 1, 2, and 5 now? I'd leave 3 (the token) and 4 (version policy) for you since they need account access and a decision.

input.new_string:
publish-npm:
    name: publish to npm
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      id-token: write # required for npm provenance via OIDC
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "22"
          cache: pnpm
          registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: Build ESM bundle
        run: pnpm build

      - name: Publish to npm
        # Version comes from package.json — bump it before tagging. npm exits 409
        # if this version was already published, which fails the job loudly.
        run: npm publish --provenance --access public
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}

  release:
    name: publish release
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:

Clean — the package is now 19.1 KB with just `dist/cli.js`, `bin/assert.js`, `README`, `LICENSE`, and `package.json`.

## Done

**`package.json`**
- Renamed `@assert-labs/cli` → **`@assertlabs/cli`**
- Added `repository`, `homepage`, `bugs` (provenance requires `repository`)
- Added `publishConfig: { access: "public", provenance: true }`
- Narrowed `files` to `dist/cli.js` + `bin` so SEA build artifacts don't ship

**`README.md`**
- Install line → `npm install -g @assertlabs/cli`
- Added npm version + license badges (`shields.io/npm/v` and `/npm/l` for `@assertlabs/cli`) alongside the existing CI/release/Discord badges

**`.github/workflows/release.yml`**
- New `publish-npm` job: runs on every `v*` tag after `build`, with `id-token: write` for OIDC provenance, installs → builds → `npm publish --provenance --access public` using `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}`

Verified: `pnpm build` passes and `npm pack --dry-run` produces a correct `@assertlabs/cli@0.1.4` tarball.

## What's left for you (needs account access)

1. **Add the `NPM_TOKEN` secret** to the `Assert-Labs/cli` repo — create a **Granular/Automation** token at npmjs.com (Automation type bypasses 2FA in CI). While there, `npm login` then `npm org ls assertlabs` to confirm the org is yours and you're a member.
2. **First publish:** since `0.1.4` has never been published, you can do the very first one manually to claim the name if you want (`pnpm build && npm publish`), or just push a tag and let the new job do it. Either way the name gets claimed on first publish.
3. **Per release going forward:** bump `version` in `package.json`, commit, then tag `vX.Y.Z` — the workflow publishes binaries, Homebrew, and npm together. (npm returns 409 and fails the job if you forget to bump, so drift is loud rather than silent.)

Note I left the SEA-binary build job pulling the full `dist/` via the build script unchanged — narrowing `files` only affects the npm tarball, not the GitHub-release binaries.

Want me to also update the docs site (`docs/` / docs.assert.dev) to the new name, or are you handling that separately?

content: Instead of an NPM_TOKEN, shouldn't I use Trusted Publishing instead?

text: Good call — yes, npm Trusted Publishing (GA since mid-2025) is the better choice here. Let me verify the current specifics before I rewrite the workflow.

input.prompt: What are the requirements and setup steps for npm trusted publishing from GitHub Actions? Specifically: minimum npm CLI version required, whether a package must already exist before configuring a trusted publisher (first-publish limitation), what GitHub Actions permissions are needed, whether NODE_AUTH_TOKEN is still needed, and whether provenance is automatic.

output:
# GitHub Actions Trusted Publishing Requirements

**Minimum npm CLI Version:** "Trusted publishing requires npm CLI version 11.5.1 or later and Node version 22.14.0 or higher."

**First-Publish Limitation:** The documentation does not address whether a package must exist beforehand. This requirement is not mentioned in the provided content.

**Required GitHub Actions Permissions:** The workflow needs the `id-token: write` permission to enable OIDC token generation. The documentation states: "The critical requirement is the `id-token: write` permission, which allows GitHub Actions to generate OIDC tokens."

**NODE_AUTH_TOKEN for Publishing:** Not required for the publish operation itself when using trusted publishing. However, if your project has private dependencies, you should still use a read-only token for the install/ci step: "Always use read-only granular access tokens for installing dependencies. This limits potential damage if the token is ever compromised."

**Automatic Provenance:** Yes, provenance generation is automatic when publishing via GitHub Actions with trusted publishing. The documentation explains: "When you publish using trusted publishing from GitHub Actions or GitLab CI/CD, npm automatically generates and publishes provenance attestations for your package. This happens by default—you don't need to add the `--provenance` flag."

text: Let me confirm the first-publish situation, since `@assertlabs/cli` doesn't exist yet — that's the one gotcha for a brand-new package.

input.query: npm trusted publishing new package must exist first publish chicken and egg configure trusted publisher

output:

## Search Results

I found relevant information about npm trusted publishing and the "chicken and egg" problem you're referring to. Here are the key findings:

### The Chicken and Egg Problem

Unlike PyPI which allows configuring OIDC for not-yet-existing packages, npm requires a package to exist before you can configure trusted publishing. This creates a circular dependency issue for new packages.

### The Solution

A tool called `setup-npm-trusted-publish` helps work around this limitation by creating and publishing a minimal placeholder package. The workflow is:

1. Run the tool to create and publish a placeholder package
2. Visit the provided URL to configure OIDC trusted publishing
3. Set up your CI/CD workflow to publish the real package version

### Alternative: npm trust Command

More recently, maintainers can now add or update trusted publishing configurations across multiple packages in a single operation using the npm trust command instead of configuring each package individually. However, npm 11.10.0+ provides an npm trust command that can configure trusted publishing without publishing a placeholder, but Granular Access Tokens with the bypass 2FA option are not supported.

### Configuration Steps

Navigate to your package settings on npmjs.com and find the "Trusted Publisher" section. Under "Select your publisher", choose your CI/CD provider by clicking the GitHub Actions, GitLab CI/CD, or CircleCI button.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

text:
Confirmed. Trusted Publishing is the better path — no long-lived secret, and provenance becomes automatic. One real catch for you: **npm (unlike PyPI) requires the package to already exist before you can configure a trusted publisher**, so `@assertlabs/cli` needs one manual first publish to claim the name; every release after that goes tokenless through Actions.

Let me rewrite the job accordingly.

input.old_string:
publish-npm:
    name: publish to npm
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      id-token: write # required for npm provenance via OIDC
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "22"
          cache: pnpm
          registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: Build ESM bundle
        run: pnpm build

      - name: Publish to npm
        # Version comes from package.json — bump it before tagging. npm exits 409
        # if this version was already published, which fails the job loudly.
        run: npm publish --provenance --access public
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}
input.new_string:
publish-npm:
    name: publish to npm
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      id-token: write # OIDC token for npm trusted publishing (no NPM_TOKEN needed)
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "22"
          cache: pnpm
          registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org

      # Trusted publishing requires npm >= 11.5.1; Node 22 still bundles npm 10.x.
      - name: Upgrade npm for trusted publishing
        run: npm install -g npm@latest

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: Build ESM bundle
        run: pnpm build

      - name: Publish to npm
        # Authenticated via OIDC trusted publishing (configured on the npm package
        # settings page) — no token. Provenance is generated automatically, so no
        # --provenance flag. Version comes from package.json; bump it before
        # tagging or npm exits 409 and fails the job loudly.
        run: npm publish --access public

text:
Done. Trusted Publishing is now wired in. Summary of what changed from the token approach:

**`release.yml` — `publish-npm` job**
- Removed `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN` / `secrets.NPM_TOKEN` entirely
- Kept `id-token: write` (this is what OIDC trusted publishing uses)
- Added an `npm install -g npm@latest` step — trusted publishing needs **npm ≥ 11.5.1**, and the Node 22 runner only bundles npm 10.x
- `npm publish --access public` (dropped `--provenance` — it's automatic under trusted publishing)

**`package.json`**
- Removed `publishConfig.provenance: true` (redundant; provenance is generated automatically)
- Kept `access: public` and the `repository` field (the latter is what makes the provenance/source link show up on npm)

## Your manual steps now (no token to create)

1. **First publish to claim the name** — npm requires the package to exist before a trusted publisher can be configured (the [chicken-and-egg](https://docs.npmjs.com/trusted-publishers/) that PyPI doesn't have). Two options:
   - Simplest: `npm login` then locally `pnpm build && npm publish` once for `0.1.4`.
   - Or, on npm ≥ 11.10.0, use [`npm trust`](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v11/commands/npm-trust/) to configure the publisher without a placeholder.
2. **Configure the trusted publisher** on npmjs.com → package **@assertlabs/cli** → Settings → **Trusted Publisher** → GitHub Actions. Fill in:
   - Organization/repo: `Assert-Labs/cli`
   - Workflow filename: `release.yml`
   - (Environment: leave blank unless you add one to the job)
3. **Per release:** bump `version` in `package.json`, commit, tag `vX.Y.Z` — the workflow publishes binaries, Homebrew, and npm tokenlessly, with provenance attached automatically.

The README/badges and the package name/contents from before are unchanged and still correct.

Sources: [npm trusted publishers docs](https://docs.npmjs.com/trusted-publishers/), [npm trust command](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v11/commands/npm-trust/), [GitHub changelog: bulk trusted publishing](https://github.blog/changelog/2026-02-18-npm-bulk-trusted-publishing-config-and-script-security-now-generally-available/)

npm notice Publishing to https://registry.npmjs.org/ with tag latest and public access
npm error code E403
npm error 403 403 Forbidden - PUT https://registry.npmjs.org/@assertlabs%2fcli - Two-factor authentication or granular access token with bypass 2fa enabled is required to publish packages.

text:
That 403 is an account/auth issue, not a problem with the package config — npm requires 2FA (or a bypass-2FA token) to publish, and your plain `npm publish` didn't supply a one-time code. Pick one:

**Option A — supply your 2FA code (simplest, recommended for this one-time bootstrap)**

If you have an authenticator app on the npm account, just pass the current 6-digit code:

```bash
pnpm build && npm publish --access public --otp=123456
```

(Interactive publish *should* prompt for the OTP, but many shells don't get the prompt — passing `--otp` explicitly is the reliable fix.)

If you don't have 2FA set up yet, enable it first at **npmjs.com → Account → Two-Factor Authentication** (authenticator app), then run the command above.

**Option B — one-time token with bypass-2FA**

Create a **Granular Access Token** at npmjs.com with "Bypass two-factor authentication" enabled (scoped to `@assertlabs/cli` / read-write), then:

```bash
NPM_CONFIG_//registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=<token> npm publish --access public
```

Either way, this is **only for the very first publish** to claim the name. After that, set up the Trusted Publisher on the package settings page and every release goes through Actions tokenless — no OTP, no token.

Note one thing the earlier research flagged: if you later want to use the `npm trust` *command* to configure the publisher, granular tokens with bypass-2FA aren't supported for that — but it doesn't matter here since you'll configure the trusted publisher through the **website UI** after this first publish. Option A keeps things cleanest.
